title: How much does it cost to run an electric radiator?
description: How much does it cost to run an electric radiator in the UK? Per-hour, per-evening and winter costs at the July 2026 price cap, plus how it compares with gas.
blurb: What an electric radiator really costs per hour, per evening and over a winter, and how to cut the bill.
lede: An electric radiator is the simplest heating there is: plug it in, turn the dial, and every watt it draws becomes heat in the room. That simplicity makes the cost easy to work out, but also easy to get wrong, because the number printed on the box is the peak draw, not what the radiator actually uses once the thermostat starts cycling. This guide gives you the real per-hour figures at the current price cap, shows how an evening or a whole winter of use adds up, and explains exactly when an electric radiator beats gas central heating and when it loses badly.
short_answer: A typical 1,500W electric radiator costs about 39p per hour running flat out at the July to September 2026 price cap of 26.11p per kWh. In practice the built-in thermostat switches the element off once the room is warm, so a realistic figure is 20p to 25p per hour after warm-up. A four-hour evening usually costs around £1, and heating one room every evening through winter lands somewhere between £100 and £150.

sections.h2: What an electric radiator costs per hour
sections.html:
The maths is the friendliest of any appliance. An electric radiator converts all of its electricity into heat, so the cost per hour at full power is simply the wattage in kilowatts multiplied by your unit rate. A 2kW radiator running flat out for an hour uses 2 kWh, which costs 52p at the current cap.

The table below shows the flat-out cost per hour for common sizes, and then two more useful numbers: what a four-hour evening typically costs once the thermostat is cycling (assuming the element is actually on about 60% of the time), and what a week of those evenings adds up to.

Radiator sizeCost per hour (flat out)Typical 4-hour evening7 evenings a week
500W (small panel)13p31p£2.19
800W (bedroom size)21p50p£3.51
1,000W26p63p£4.39
1,500W (typical living room)39p94p£6.58
2,000W52p£1.25£8.77
2,500W (large oil-filled)65p£1.57£10.97

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Evening and weekly figures assume the thermostat keeps the element on for 60% of the time after warm-up.

Stretch that 1,500W living-room example across a heating season of roughly 150 days and you get about 540 kWh, which is £141 at the current rate. That is the realistic annual cost of using an electric radiator as your main evening heat source for one room. The same kilowatts-times-rate method works for any appliance in the house, but heaters are where it matters most because the wattages are so large.

sections.h2: Why the wattage on the box overstates the real cost
sections.html:
Almost every electric radiator sold in the UK has a thermostat, and since 2018 the Ecodesign rules (often called Lot 20) have required fixed electric heaters to include proper temperature control and timers. That changes the sums in your favour.

When you first switch the radiator on in a cold room, it does run flat out, and a 2kW unit really does cost 52p for that first hour. But once the room reaches the set temperature, the thermostat starts switching the element off and on to hold it there. In a reasonably insulated room the element might only be live for half the time, so the average draw of that 2kW radiator falls to around 1kW and the cost to roughly 26p per hour.

Three things decide how often the element has to fire:

  • Insulation and draughts. A leaky room loses heat faster, so the element stays on longer. Cheap draught-proofing pays for itself fastest in rooms heated by electricity.
  • Room size relative to the radiator. An undersized radiator in a big room never reaches the set point and runs continuously at the flat-out price. As a rough rule you want about 100W per square metre of floor in an average room, more in a poorly insulated one.
  • Your set temperature. Each degree lower cuts consumption by roughly 6 to 10%, the same rule of thumb that applies to central heating.

So the honest answer to the per-hour question is a range. A 1,500W radiator costs 39p per hour worst case, and more like 20p to 25p per hour once the room is warm and the thermostat is doing its job.

sections.h2: Electric radiator vs gas central heating
sections.html:
This is the comparison that decides whether an electric radiator is a money-saver or a money pit. At the current cap, electricity costs 26.11p per kWh and gas costs 7.33p per kWh. A modern condensing boiler turns roughly 90% of that gas into useful heat, so a kWh of warmth from your central heating costs about 8.1p. The same kWh of warmth from an electric radiator costs 26.11p, more than three times as much.

Per unit of heat, gas wins by a wide margin, and if you tried to heat a whole house on plug-in electric radiators your bill would be brutal. But the comparison most people actually face is different: one electric radiator in one room versus firing up the boiler for the whole house.

  • A 1,500W electric radiator warming your living room for a four-hour evening uses about 3.6 kWh and costs roughly 94p.
  • Running gas central heating for the same evening in a typical three-bed house might burn 15 to 25 kWh of gas, costing £1.10 to £1.83, and it heats rooms you may not be using.

So if you genuinely only need one room warm, the electric radiator can come out level or slightly ahead despite the much higher unit rate. The moment you need two or three rooms warm, gas pulls ahead decisively. We work through the full sums in whether it is cheaper to heat one room or the whole house.

One caveat: if your home has no gas supply at all, the comparison changes completely, and options like storage heaters on an off-peak tariff or a heat pump become the relevant alternatives rather than a boiler.

sections.h2: Does the type of electric radiator matter?
sections.html:
Here is the fact the marketing departments would rather you did not know: every plug-in electric heater is 100% efficient at turning electricity into heat. An oil-filled radiator, a dry thermal element radiator, a ceramic panel, a glass-fronted designer model and a £15 fan heater all deliver exactly one kWh of heat for one kWh of electricity. No electric radiator can be more efficient than another, so claims of "eco" elements or special heat-retaining cores cutting your bills by 30% do not survive contact with physics.

What does differ is how the heat is delivered, and that can change how much you need:

  • Oil-filled radiators heat up slowly and keep radiating after the thermostat cuts power, which gives steady, comfortable background warmth. Good for rooms used for hours at a stretch.
  • Panel and convector radiators warm the air quickly but cool quickly too. Better for short bursts.
  • Infrared panels heat people and surfaces directly rather than the air, which can let you feel warm at a lower air temperature. That is the one genuine route to using fewer kWh.
  • Smart radiators with app control and open-window detection do not heat more efficiently, but better scheduling means fewer wasted hours, which is where their real saving lives.

The other genuine differences are controls and accuracy. A radiator with a precise electronic thermostat and a proper timer wastes less than one with a vague mechanical dial, because it avoids overshooting the temperature you asked for. Note that electric heaters do not carry the A-to-G rating you see on fridges and washing machines, so do not go hunting for one; our guide to energy labels explains which products are covered and why. For a fuller comparison of the options, see electric heater types compared.

sections.h2: How to cut the cost of running an electric radiator
sections.html:
Because the unit rate is high, small habits move the bill more than they do with gas. The savings below are listed roughly in order of impact.

  • Size the radiator to the room and use the thermostat. Set a target temperature rather than running on full. Every degree you shave saves roughly 6 to 10% of the radiator's consumption.
  • Heat the person, not the building. If you are sat still working or watching television, a 120W electric blanket or heated throw costs about 3p per hour against 39p for a 1,500W radiator. Combining a low room temperature with direct warmth is the single biggest saving available.
  • Use the timer. An electric radiator heats a room quickly, so there is no need to run it for an hour before you arrive. Fifteen to twenty minutes of warm-up is plenty in most rooms.
  • Close the door and block the draughts. An electric radiator heating a room with the door open is paying 26p per kWh to warm the hallway.
  • Check your tariff. If you are on Economy 7, your daytime rate is usually higher than the standard cap rate, which makes daytime electric heating even dearer; night-rate tariffs only help if you can shift heating to the off-peak window, which is what storage heaters are for.
  • Switch it off at the wall when the season ends. A basic radiator draws nothing when off, but smart models with wifi and standby electronics trickle a few watts all year. It is small, but it is the same leak we cover in standby power, the full story, and a plug-in energy monitor will show you in seconds whether yours is one of the guilty ones.

An electric radiator costs its wattage in kilowatts times 26.11p for every hour it runs flat out, so 13p per hour for a small 500W panel and 52p for a 2kW model at the current cap. Real-world costs are lower once the thermostat is cycling: budget around £1 for a four-hour evening with a 1,500W radiator, £6 to £7 a week for nightly use, and £100 to £150 across a winter for one well-controlled room.

Per unit of heat it is more than three times the price of mains gas, so it makes sense as a precision tool, not a whole-house solution. Use it to warm the one room you are actually in, keep the door shut, set the thermostat a degree lower than you think you need, and let the timer do the discipline for you. Used that way, an electric radiator is often the cheapest warm evening on offer. Used as a substitute for central heating in three rooms at once, it is one of the most expensive.

faq.q: How much does a 2kW electric radiator cost per hour?
faq.a: Running flat out, a 2kW electric radiator uses 2 kWh per hour, which costs about 52p at the July to September 2026 price cap of 26.11p per kWh. Once the room is up to temperature and the thermostat is cycling the element, the realistic cost falls to roughly 26p to 35p per hour.

faq.q: Is an electric radiator cheaper than gas central heating?
faq.a: Per unit of heat, no. Heat from a condensing gas boiler costs about 8.1p per kWh against 26.11p for electric at the current cap. But heating a single occupied room with one electric radiator can match or beat the cost of running the boiler for the whole house, so it depends entirely on how many rooms you need warm.

faq.q: How much does it cost to leave an electric radiator on all night?
faq.a: A 1,000W oil-filled radiator on a low thermostat setting overnight typically runs its element about half the time, using around 4 kWh over eight hours. That costs roughly £1.04 a night at the current cap, or about £31 a month if you do it nightly. A low-wattage electric blanket does the same comfort job for a fraction of that.

faq.q: Are oil-filled radiators cheaper to run than panel heaters?
faq.a: Not per unit of heat. All electric heaters are 100% efficient, so a kWh of warmth costs the same from any of them. Oil-filled models hold their heat and cycle more gently, which suits long sessions, while panels warm up faster for short bursts. Pick by use pattern and thermostat quality, not by efficiency claims.

faq.q: Do eco or energy-saving electric radiators use less electricity?
faq.a: The heating element itself cannot be more efficient, so an eco model produces heat at exactly the same cost per kWh as any other. What better models offer is more accurate thermostats, timers and features like open-window detection, which avoid wasted hours. The saving comes from running less, not from heating more efficiently.
